states.name: Kirin
states.title: Mythweaver
states.description:
The illusive Kirin are common characters in Erden mythology, but their significance greatly differs from culture to culture. The Zuo people of Eastland believe that the appearance of a Kirin is a sign that a god is near, whilst the legends of the Akashi people depict the Kirin as a symbol of great happiness.

Few have ever met a Kirin, and reports of their nature vary from gentle and majestic to divinely terrifying.

states.name: Kirin
states.title: Mythweaver
states.description: An ancient folktale found among various cultural groups in Westland tells the story of a child abandoned at birth and raised by a Kirin. The child, in some tales called Enkidu and by others Eabani, grew up in the spirit world compassionate and gentle. When she turned 16, the Kirin took her back to the material realm, where she was greatly saddened by the division between its peoples. She begged her foster parent to do something, and moved by his ward's conviction, the Kirin led a procession of his breathren to thunder across the night sky.

states.name: Kirin
states.title: Mythweaver
states.description:
The people of Westland looked up in wonder to see the procession of Kirin marching across the heavens. Their hooves left a trail of golden dust in their wake, which remained in the sky for seven days and seven nights after the Kirin depearted.

It is said that the scattered peoples of Ancient Westland made a ceasefire treaty effective every year for these seven days and seven nights. Many scholars equate this week of peaceful celebration with the modern day Helvati Ker festivities observed by many groups in Westland's northwest.
